attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old man presents to the hospital with a three-week history of worsening bilateral leg tenderness and weakness that today resulted in a fall. This is in the context of a six-month history of haemoptysis, weight loss and anorexia. He is continent of his bladder and bowels. His medical history includes melanoma (excised), chronic back pain, hypertension, and benign prostatic hypertrophy. He is an ex-smoker.

On examination, there are crepitations present throughout his lung fields, wasting of his right thenar eminence, and bilateral ptosis. Neurological examination of the lower limb demonstrates reduced power 4/5 primarily in proximal muscle groups with reduced reflexes. Post-tetanic potentiation is present. The anal tone is intact.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the likely underlying pathology?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's history and examination are consistent with Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome with a lung primary malignancy, which is most likely small cell lung cancer. Lung adenocarcinoma is less likely to be associated with Lambert-Eaton syndrome. Metastatic prostate cancer with spinal metastasis or B12 deficiency with subacute combined degeneration of the cord may also cause neurological symptoms but is not consistent with the type of deficit and the distribution described in this patient.

Lambert-Eaton syndrome is a rare autoimmune disorder of the neuromuscular junction associated with small cell lung carcinoma in more than 50% of cases. In Lambert-Eaton syndrome, antibodies are produced against voltage-gated calcium channel (VGCC), which reduces the activity of VGCC leading to the neurological manifestations.

The most frequent clinical manifestations of Lambert-Eaton syndrome are proximal muscle weakness, autonomic dysfunction and absent deep tendon reflexes. Oculobulbar weakness is also common, seen in 70% of patients, with ocular symptoms of ptosis and diplopia the most common cranial nerve manifestations. Autonomic dysfunction is reported in 80-96% of patients, with a dry mouth the most commonly reported symptom.

The clinical features consistent with Lambert-Eaton syndrome should prompt a thorough workup including a radiological screen for malignancy due to the strong association with small cell lung cancer.

Treatment of Lambert-Eaton syndrome involves treatment of the underlying malignancy.
learningPoints: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome can present as a paraneoplastic phenomenon and more than half of the cases are associated with small cell lung cancer.
references: Jayarangaiah A, Theetha Kariyanna P. Lambert Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome.  StatPearls. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing Copyright © 2020, StatPearls Publishing LLC.; 2020.
Kesner VG, Oh SJ, Dimachkie MM, Barohn RJ. Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome. Neurol Clin. 2018;36(2):379-94.
